content:
# Ruth Chapter 3 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 881-882 | **Source Image:** RUT_003_page_001.jpg, RUT_003_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And Naomi, her mother-in-law, said to her, "My daughter, shall I not seek rest for you, that it may be well with you?

**2** And now, is not Boaz our kinsman, with whose young women you have been? Behold, he is winnowing barley at the threshing floor tonight.

**3** Therefore wash yourself and anoint yourself, and put on your garments and go down to the threshing floor. Do not make yourself known to the man until he has finished eating and drinking.

**4** And it shall be, when he lies down, that you shall mark the place where he lies, and you shall go in and uncover his feet and lie down. And he will tell you what you should do."

**5** And she said to her, "All that you say to me I will do."

**6** So she went down to the threshing floor and did according to all that her mother-in-law had commanded her.

**7** And Boaz ate and drank, and his heart was merry. And he went to lie down at the end of the heap of grain. And she came softly and uncovered his feet and lay down.

**8** And it came to pass at midnight that the man trembled and turned himself, and behold, a woman was lying at his feet.

**9** And he said, "Who are you?" And she said, "I am Ruth, your maidservant. Spread your wing over your maidservant, for you are a kinsman-redeemer."

**10** And he said, "Blessed are you of the LORD, my daughter. You have made your latter act of faithful love greater than the first, in that you have not gone after the young men, whether poor or rich.

**11** And now, my daughter, do not fear. All that you say I will do for you, for all the gate of my people knows that you are a woman of valor.

**12** And now, it is true indeed that I am a kinsman-redeemer, yet there is a kinsman-redeemer nearer than I.

**13** Lodge here tonight, and it shall be in the morning: if he will redeem you, good -- let him redeem. But if he is not willing to redeem you, then I will redeem you, as the LORD lives. Lie down until the morning."

**14** And she lay at his feet until the morning, and she arose before one could recognize another. And he said, "Let it not be known that the woman came to the threshing floor."

**15** And he said, "Bring the shawl that is upon you and hold it." And she held it, and he measured out six measures of barley and placed it upon her. And he went into the city.

**16** And she came to her mother-in-law, and she said, "How did it go, my daughter?" And she told her all that the man had done for her.

**17** And she said, "These six measures of barley he gave to me, for he said to me, 'Do not go empty-handed to your mother-in-law.'"

**18** And she said, "Sit still, my daughter, until you know how the matter will fall, for the man will not rest unless he has settled the matter today."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 3 is the dramatic and theological center of Ruth. The threshing floor encounter is narrated with exquisite restraint in the Hebrew, and SOPHIA has endeavored to preserve that restraint in the consensus.

Manuscript verification: The Leningrad Codex images (pages 881-882) confirm the text. The chapter division is not marked in the manuscript but the narrative shift is clearly delineated by the Masoretic petuhah/setumah spacing.

Verses where all three substantially agreed: 2, 4, 5, 6, 8, 13 (core content), 14, 17. In these cases SOPHIA adopted the formal-equivalent rendering.

Key term decisions for this chapter:
- **kanaf** (3:9): Rendered "wing" -- preserving the deliberate echo with 2:12 (God's "wings"). All three translators recognize this connection, but PNEUMA renders it "cloak," which collapses the divine-human parallelism.
- **go'el** (3:9, 12, 13): Rendered "kinsman-redeemer" consistently.
- **hesed** (3:10): Rendered "faithful love" -- consistent with chapters 1 and 2.
- **eshet hayil** (3:11): Rendered "woman of valor" -- see disagreement log below.

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 1 -- "rest" vs. "a home where you will be provided for"
- **EMET:** "shall-I-not seek for-you rest"
- **LOGOS:** "shall I not seek rest for you"
- **PNEUMA:** "shouldn't I find you a home where you will be provided for"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "shall I not seek rest for you." The Hebrew manoah means "rest" or "settled security." PNEUMA's interpretive expansion ("a home where you will be provided for") makes explicit what the Hebrew leaves compact. The theological nuance of "rest" -- connoting not just physical shelter but the settled peace of covenant belonging -- is better served by the literal term.

### Verse 2 -- "our kinsman" vs. "our relative"
- **EMET:** "Boaz our-kinsman, whom you-were with his-young-women"
- **LOGOS:** "Boaz our kinsman, with whose young women you have been"
- **PNEUMA:** "Boaz, whose young women you have been working alongside, is our relative"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew moda'atenu ("our kinsman/acquaintance") is rendered "kinsman" for consistency. PNEUMA's "relative" is weaker and her sentence reordering changes the emphasis.

### Verse 3 -- "anoint yourself" vs. "put on perfume" and "your garments" vs. "your best clothes"
- **EMET:** "you-shall-anoint-yourself, and-you-shall-put your-garments upon-you"
- **LOGOS:** "anoint yourself, and put on your garments"
- **PNEUMA:** "put on perfume, and dress in your best clothes"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Anoint" renders the Hebrew sakht precisely (it connotes oil, not modern perfume). "Your garments" preserves the Hebrew simlotayikh without PNEUMA's interpretive addition of "best" -- the text does not say her finest clothes, merely her garments (possibly her going-out clothes, but the Hebrew does not specify quality).

### Verse 7 -- "in secret" vs. "softly" vs. "quietly"
- **EMET:** "she-came in-secret"
- **LOGOS:** "she came softly"
- **PNEUMA:** "Ruth came quietly"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "softly." The Hebrew ballat means "in secret" or "stealthily" or "softly." "Softly" captures the physical manner without the conspiratorial overtone of EMET's "in secret" or the flatness of PNEUMA's "quietly."

### Verse 7 -- "at the end of the heap" vs. "at the far end of the grain pile"
- **EMET:** "at-[the]-end of-the-heap"
- **LOGOS:** "at the end of the heap of grain"
- **PNEUMA:** "at the far end of the grain pile"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Heap of grain" clarifies the Hebrew ha'aremah. PNEUMA's "far end" adds a spatial specificity not in the text, and "grain pile" is too informal.

### Verse 8 -- "trembled" vs. "was startled"
- **EMET:** "trembled the-man"
- **LOGOS:** "the man trembled"
- **PNEUMA:** "the man was startled"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "trembled." The Hebrew vayyeherad specifically means to tremble or shudder, a physical response. PNEUMA's "was startled" shifts to a cognitive response.

### Verse 9 -- kanaf: "wing" vs. "cloak"
- **EMET:** "you-shall-spread your-wing [kanaf] over your-maidservant"
- **LOGOS:** "Spread your wing over your maidservant"
- **PNEUMA:** "Spread your cloak over me"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "Spread your wing over your maidservant." This is a critical decision. The Hebrew kenafekha means "your wing" -- the same root as kenafav ("his wings") in 2:12, where Boaz prayed Ruth would find refuge under God's wings. Ruth now asks Boaz to be the human agent of that divine covering. PNEUMA's "cloak" collapses this theological echo. While kanaf can mean "garment-edge," the primary meaning "wing" must be preserved to maintain the thematic arc. Additionally, PNEUMA drops "your maidservant" (amatekha) in favor of "me," losing Ruth's self-effacing legal language.

### Verse 9 -- go'el: "kinsman-redeemer" vs. "family redeemer"
- **EMET:** "a-kinsman-redeemer [go'el]"
- **LOGOS:** "a kinsman-redeemer"
- **PNEUMA:** "a family redeemer"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "kinsman-redeemer" -- consistent throughout.

### Verse 10 -- hesed: "lovingkindness" vs. "faithful love" vs. "act of loyalty"
- **EMET:** "your-latter lovingkindness [hesed] more-than the-former"
- **LOGOS:** "your latter act of faithful love greater than the first"
- **PNEUMA:** "This act of loyalty surpasses even your first"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "your latter act of faithful love greater than the first." PNEUMA's "act of loyalty" narrows hesed to mere loyalty. EMET's "lovingkindness" is consistent within EMET but SOPHIA's standard is "faithful love."

### Verse 11 -- eshet hayil: "woman of valor" vs. "woman of worth" vs. "woman of noble character"
- **EMET:** "a-woman of-valor [eshet hayil]"
- **LOGOS:** "a woman of worth"
- **PNEUMA:** "a woman of noble character"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "woman of valor" (EMET). This is a deliberate departure from LOGOS. The Hebrew hayil is the same word used of Boaz in 2:1 (gibbor hayil, "great worth/valor"). "Valor" preserves the martial-heroic overtones of the root and creates the strongest echo with Boaz's characterization. It also matches the traditional rendering of eshet hayil in Proverbs 31:10. LOGOS's "worth" is defensible but less resonant; PNEUMA's "noble character" is an interpretive gloss.

### Verse 12 -- "indeed truly" vs. "it is true that" vs. "it is true that"
- **EMET:** "indeed truly, I [am] a-kinsman-redeemer"
- **LOGOS:** "it is true that I am a kinsman-redeemer"
- **PNEUMA:** "it is true that I am a redeemer"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA combines: "it is true indeed" -- preserving the emphatic double particle (ki omnam) that EMET captures with "indeed truly" while using LOGOS's more natural English syntax. PNEUMA drops "kinsman-" from the term.

### Verse 15 -- "and he went into the city" (Ketiv/Qere issue)
- **EMET:** "And-he-went [to] the-city" (follows Leningrad masculine Ketiv)
- **LOGOS:** "And he went into the city" (follows Leningrad masculine reading)
- **PNEUMA:** "she went into the town" (follows alternative feminine reading)
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "And he went into the city." The Leningrad Codex reads vayyavo' (masculine), and all translators acknowledge this. SOPHIA follows the Codex text. PNEUMA's feminine reading follows a different textual tradition not supported by the primary source manuscript.

### Verse 16 -- "Who are you?" vs. "How did it go?"
- **EMET:** "Who [are] you, my-daughter?" (literal)
- **LOGOS:** "How did it go with you, my daughter?" (interpretive)
- **PNEUMA:** "How did it go, my daughter?" (interpretive)
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "How did it go, my daughter?" The Hebrew mi att ("who are you") is widely understood as an idiomatic inquiry about what happened rather than a literal identity question (as EMET's own note acknowledges). In the darkness of pre-dawn, it functions as "What news?" LOGOS and PNEUMA correctly render the sense.

### Verse 17 -- "empty" vs. "empty-handed"
- **EMET:** "Do-not-go empty to your-mother-in-law"
- **LOGOS:** "Do not go empty-handed to your mother-in-law"
- **PNEUMA:** "Don't go back to your mother-in-law empty-handed"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "empty-handed." The Hebrew reqam means "empty" or "empty-handed." "Empty-handed" makes the physical sense clear and echoes Naomi's lament in 1:21 ("the LORD has brought me back empty"). EMET's bare "empty" works but is less idiomatic in English.

### Verse 18 -- "Sit" vs. "Sit still" vs. "Sit tight"
- **EMET:** "Sit, my-daughter"
- **LOGOS:** "Sit still, my daughter"
- **PNEUMA:** "Sit tight, my daughter"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "Sit still." The Hebrew shevi means "sit/remain." "Sit still" captures the sense of patient waiting that Naomi counsels. PNEUMA's "Sit tight" is too colloquial.

### Verse 18 -- "the matter will fall" vs. "how this turns out"
- **EMET:** "how the-matter will-fall"
- **LOGOS:** "how the matter will fall"
- **PNEUMA:** "how this turns out"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "how the matter will fall" -- preserving the Hebrew idiom (yippol davar, literally "the matter will fall"). PNEUMA's "turns out" loses the vivid imagery.

---

*Signed, SOPHIA*
